title: Condensation and mould: the cheap fixes
description: Stop condensation and mould without big spending: free habits, fixes under £20, and real UK running costs for extractor fans, dehumidifiers and PIV units.
blurb: Black mould on the windowsill? The cheapest fixes are habits, not gadgets. Here is what actually works and what it costs.
lede: Black spots creeping across the bathroom ceiling, water pooling on the windowsill every morning, that musty smell in the bedroom corner. Condensation and the mould that follows it are the most common damp problem in UK homes, and the good news is that most cases are fixed with habits and kit costing pennies, not a builder. The cause is simple physics: warm indoor air holds moisture, and when it touches a cold surface such as single glazing or an uninsulated external wall, the moisture condenses out as liquid water. Mould then feeds on that water. Cut the moisture, move the air, or warm the surface, and the problem retreats. This guide works through the fixes in price order, from free to about £20, with honest running costs for the powered options.
short_answer: Most condensation problems are solved by producing less moisture and ventilating more, which costs almost nothing. Running a bathroom extractor fan for 30 minutes after every shower costs about 0.3p a time, roughly £1.20 a year, and is the single highest-impact cheap fix. A compressor dehumidifier costs around 5p an hour if you need heavier lifting. Aim to keep indoor relative humidity between 40 and 60 per cent; a £5 hygrometer tells you where you stand.

sections.h2: Why your home gets condensation in the first place
sections.html:
An average household puts a surprising amount of water into the air every day. Cooking with uncovered pans releases up to 3 litres, a single load of washing dried indoors gives off around 2 litres, showers and baths add another litre or two, and four sleeping people breathe out roughly 2 litres overnight between them. A busy family home can easily generate 10 litres of airborne moisture a day.

That moisture has to go somewhere. In an older, leakier house it used to escape through chimneys, gappy windows and floorboards. Modern homes, and older homes that have been sensibly sealed up with draught-proofing and double glazing, hold onto both heat and moisture. The heat is welcome. The moisture is not, and without deliberate ventilation it ends up on your coldest surfaces: window glass and frames, the corners of external walls, and behind furniture against outside walls.

Mould needs sustained surface moisture to grow, which is why it appears exactly where condensation forms repeatedly. Fix the condensation and you starve the mould. The three levers are always the same: produce less moisture, ventilate moisture out at source, and keep surfaces warm enough that water vapour does not condense on them. Cold rooms make all three worse, one reason a home can feel damp and chilly even with the heating running.

sections.h2: The free fixes: habits that remove most of the problem
sections.html:
Before spending anything, change where the moisture goes. These cost nothing and together they deal with the bulk of typical condensation:

  • Lids on pans. Cooking with lids cuts steam dramatically and the food cooks faster, saving a little energy too.
  • Close the kitchen and bathroom doors while cooking or showering, and for 20 to 30 minutes afterwards. This traps moisture in the room where it is made so it can be vented out instead of drifting into cold bedrooms.
  • Open the window after a shower. Ten minutes with the door shut clears most of the steam. In winter this loses far less heat than people fear; you are venting one small room briefly, not chilling the whole house.
  • Dry washing outdoors or in one ventilated room. Indoor drying on radiators is the classic mould trigger. If you must dry inside, use one room with the door closed and the window cracked, or see drying clothes without a tumble dryer for cheaper options that do not soak your air.
  • Vent the tumble dryer properly. A loose hose dumps litres of water indoors per load.
  • Pull furniture 5 to 10 cm off external walls. Air needs to circulate behind wardrobes and beds, otherwise you get a cold, still pocket where mould thrives unseen.
  • Wipe windows and sills each morning. Wring the cloth out down the sink so the water does not evaporate back into the room.
  • Open trickle vents if your windows have them. They exist precisely for this job and the heat loss is small.

One habit to be careful with: drying the house out by turning the heating off entirely makes things worse, not better. Cold air holds less moisture, so relative humidity rises and surfaces get colder. Low, steady background heat in problem rooms beats blasts of heat followed by cold spells.

sections.h2: Fixes under £20 that punch above their weight
sections.html:
A small amount of money buys a lot of progress here:

  • A hygrometer, £5 to £10. Turns guesswork into numbers: aim for 40 to 60 per cent relative humidity; sustained readings above 65 to 70 per cent mean mould is likely. Move it room to room to find problem areas.
  • Moisture absorber pots, £2 to £5 each. Calcium chloride traps that pull water from the air. They cannot fix a whole damp house, but they are genuinely useful in enclosed dead spots: wardrobes, cupboards on external walls, caravans, rarely used rooms.
  • Fungicidal mould wash, £5 to £8. Kills mould rather than just smearing it around. More on safe cleaning below.
  • Anti-mould paint additive or bathroom paint, £10 to £20. Once a wall or ceiling is cleaned and dry, a fungicidal paint slows regrowth considerably in bathrooms and kitchens.
  • Secondary glazing film, £10 to £15 per window. Shrink-fit film adds an insulating air gap to single glazing, warming the inner glass surface so less moisture condenses on it. It also cuts heat loss.
  • A squeegee, £3. Unglamorous, effective, lasts years.

What not to buy first: a dehumidifier. They work, but many households that ventilate properly and fix their drying habits never need the £100 to £200 outlay. Check your hygrometer after two weeks of better habits before deciding.

sections.h2: What the powered fixes cost to run
sections.html:
The electric options are cheaper to run than most people assume, and far cheaper than the redecorating bill mould eventually causes. Figures below use typical wattages; check your own appliance label.

FixTypical powerTypical useCost per dayCost per year
Bathroom extractor fan25W30 min after each shower0.3p£1.19
Cooker hood (vented outside)100W30 min a day1.3p£4.77
Compressor dehumidifier200W4 hours a day20.9p£76 if used daily
Desiccant dehumidifier600W4 hours a day62.7p£229 if used daily
PIV unit (whole house)10WContinuous6.3p£23
Squeegee and morning wipe-downNoneDailyFreeFree

At 26.11p per kWh, Ofgem price cap July to September 2026.

A few things stand out. The bathroom extractor is almost free to run, so there is no reason to switch it off at the isolator to save money; the saving is about £1 a year and the mould risk is real. If you do need a dehumidifier, a compressor model costs around 5p an hour and pays for itself in avoided redecorating; see dehumidifier running costs for sizing and practicalities. Desiccant models cost roughly three times as much per hour but suit cold spaces. A whole-house positive input ventilation unit costs around £23 a year to run, though installation takes it well past the cheap-fix bracket.

sections.h2: Cleaning the mould you already have, safely
sections.html:
Killing existing mould matters because established colonies release spores that seed new patches. The NHS advice is that you can deal with it yourself when the affected area is small, roughly less than a square metre, and the cause is condensation rather than a leak or rising damp.

  • Wear rubber gloves and ideally a mask, and open the window while you work.
  • Wipe the mould off with a cloth dipped in soapy water or a fungicidal wash, following the label. Do not dry-brush it, which flicks spores into the air.
  • Dry the surface afterwards with a clean cloth, and bin the cloths rather than rinsing and reusing them.
  • Wash or dry-clean affected fabrics and curtains, which hold spores.
  • Once fully dry, repaint with an anti-mould paint rather than ordinary emulsion.

Neat bleach on walls is a blunt instrument: it bleaches the stain so things look fixed, but on porous surfaces it often fails to kill the roots and the mould returns. A proper fungicidal wash is a few pounds and does the job once.

The crucial point is that cleaning treats the symptom. If you scrub the mould off but change nothing about moisture and ventilation, it will be back within weeks. Pair every clean-up with at least one fix from the sections above.

sections.h2: When cheap fixes are not enough
sections.html:
Some damp is not condensation, and no amount of ventilation will fix it. Look for these signs that you have a different problem:

  • A tide mark up to about a metre on a ground floor wall suggests rising damp.
  • A damp patch that grows when it rains, often around a chimney breast, window reveal or below a gutter line, suggests penetrating damp from a leak, failed pointing or blocked gutters. Clearing gutters is itself a cheap fix worth doing first.
  • Damp under a sink, behind a bath panel or near a radiator pipe suggests a plumbing leak.

These need the source fixed, not a dehumidifier running forever to mask them. Persistent whole-house condensation in a well-ventilated home can also point to cold walls that need insulating; cavity wall insulation warms the inner wall surface and often kills corner mould as a side effect.

If you rent, damp and mould caused by the building are your landlord's responsibility to investigate and fix, and rules in this area have been tightening, particularly for social housing in England following Awaab's Law. Report problems in writing, keep photos with dates, and check the current rules and timescales on the official GOV.UK guidance, since the requirements and which landlords they cover change over time. Cheap fixes are still worth doing while you wait.

Finally, do not seal your home into a plastic bag. If you have been enthusiastic with draught-proofing, make sure purposeful ventilation remains: trickle vents open, extractors working, and ideally a read of our guide to ventilating without losing heat. The goal is airtight where you do not want airflow and deliberate ventilation where you do.

Condensation and mould respond to cheap, boring, consistent fixes far better than to expensive kit. Lids on pans, doors closed while showering, washing dried somewhere ventilated, furniture off external walls, and a wipe-down of wet windows cost nothing and remove most of the moisture load. A £5 hygrometer tells you whether it is working. The bathroom extractor fan costs about £1.20 a year to run properly, so run it every time. If readings stay above 65 per cent humidity after two weeks of better habits, a compressor dehumidifier at roughly 5p an hour is the next step, and persistent whole-house problems justify looking at PIV or insulation. Keep some background heating in problem rooms rather than letting them go cold, treat any existing mould with a fungicidal wash, and reserve the builder for damp that is not condensation at all.

faq.q: Will opening windows in winter just waste my heating?
faq.a: Short, targeted ventilation loses much less heat than people fear. Opening the bathroom window for ten minutes after a shower, with the door closed, vents a small room's worth of warm wet air. The walls and furniture hold most of the room's heat, so it recovers quickly. That brief loss is far cheaper than the redecorating, fabric damage and health effects of chronic mould. The expensive mistake is leaving windows cracked open all day across the whole house.

faq.q: Is it better to run a dehumidifier or turn up the heating?
faq.a: For removing moisture, a dehumidifier wins comfortably. Heating warms the air so it can hold more moisture, which reduces condensation on surfaces, but the water is still in your home. A 200W compressor dehumidifier costs about 5p an hour at the July to September 2026 cap rate of 26.11p per kWh and physically removes litres of water, while also returning a little heat to the room. The best combination is modest background heat plus ventilation, with a dehumidifier for stubborn rooms or indoor clothes drying.

faq.q: Why does mould keep coming back after I clean it?
faq.a: Because cleaning removes the mould but not its food supply. Mould regrows wherever a surface stays repeatedly damp, so unless you reduce moisture or warm that surface, it returns within weeks. Clean it with a fungicidal wash rather than neat bleach, dry the surface, repaint with anti-mould paint, then fix the cause: ventilate at source, stop drying washing in that room, pull furniture away from the wall, or keep the room a little warmer.

faq.q: What humidity level should my house be?
faq.a: Aim for 40 to 60 per cent relative humidity. Below 40 per cent air feels dry and can irritate skin and airways; above 60 per cent condensation risk climbs, and sustained readings over 65 to 70 per cent make mould likely on cold surfaces. A digital hygrometer costs around £5. Readings vary by room, so check bedrooms overnight and any room where you dry clothes, not just the hallway.

faq.q: Whose job is it to fix mould in a rented home?
faq.a: If the damp comes from the building itself, such as leaks, failed gutters, inadequate ventilation or cold uninsulated walls, the landlord must investigate and fix it. Rules have tightened, especially for social landlords in England under Awaab's Law, with set timescales for dealing with damp and mould hazards. Report it in writing with dated photos and check the current requirements on GOV.UK, as the rules and who they cover change. Tenants are still expected to ventilate and heat reasonably, so the cheap habit fixes help your case as well as your lungs.
